name: Radwin, Robert G.
recent_1: Konstant, A., Zhu, Z., Li, Y., Hu, Y. H., & Radwin, R. G. (2026). Modeling Human Expertise in a Sanding Task. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261460709
recent_2: Zhu, Z., Mu, F., Radwin, R. G., & Li, Y. (2025). Towards video-based injury risk assessment: predicting lifting loads from body pose trajectories. Machine Vision and Applications, 36(6). DOI: 10.1007/s00138-025-01758-w
recent_3: Konstant, A., Orr, N., Hagenow, M., Gundrum, I., Hu, Y. H., Mutlu, B., Zinn, M., Gleicher, M., & Radwin, R. G. (2024). Human–Robot Collaboration With a Corrective Shared Controlled Robot in a Sanding Task. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(3), 246-263. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241272066
recent_1_synopsis: This study characterized how skilled workers perform a manual sanding task, building a quantitative model of the motions, forces, and strategies that distinguish expert from novice performance. The goal is to encode that expertise so it can inform the design and control of collaborative robots that assist or replicate skilled manual work.
recent_2_synopsis: Using only monocular video, this work extracts body-pose trajectories during lifting and trains a Transformer model to predict the load being handled, a key contributor to low-back injury risk. It advances markerless, camera-based exposure assessment as a scalable alternative to instrumented or observational ergonomic methods.
recent_3_synopsis: This paper studied a human–robot collaborative sanding system in which a corrective shared-control robot blends the worker's intended motion with autonomous assistance to improve surface quality and reduce physical demand. It demonstrates how shared autonomy can offload the burdensome parts of a skilled manual task while keeping the human in the loop.
seminal_1: Armstrong, T. J., Radwin, R. G., Hansen, D. J., & Kennedy, K. W. (1986). Repetitive Trauma Disorders: Job Evaluation and Design. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 28(3), 325-336. DOI: 10.1177/001872088602800308
seminal_2: Pearce, M., Mutlu, B., Shah, J., & Radwin, R. (2018). Optimizing Makespan and Ergonomics in Integrating Collaborative Robots Into Manufacturing Processes. IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering, 15(4), 1772-1784. DOI: 10.1109/TASE.2018.2789820
seminal_1_synopsis: This early and influential paper laid out a framework for evaluating and redesigning jobs to reduce repetitive-trauma (cumulative-trauma) disorders by analyzing physical stressors such as force, repetition, and posture. With roughly 341 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is Radwin's most-cited work and a foundational reference linking workplace physical-exposure analysis to upper-limb musculoskeletal disorder prevention.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper formulated the integration of collaborative robots into manufacturing as a joint optimization of production efficiency (makespan) and worker ergonomic load, providing an algorithmic method to allocate tasks between human and cobot. With roughly 236 citations (Google Scholar, 2026-06-17) it is a foundational contribution bridging human factors/ergonomics and human-robot collaboration in industrial settings.
expertise_overview: Radwin's research centers on occupational ergonomics and biomechanics—quantifying workers' exposure to physical stress and understanding how force, repetition, posture, and vibration cause work-related musculoskeletal disorders and peripheral neuropathies. He develops measurement and analytical methods for assessing physical work demands, including the Strain Index, Hand Activity Level, and hand-tool and grip biomechanics. A major and growing thread is computer-vision and machine-learning approaches that automatically extract posture, motion, lifting load, and hand-activity metrics from ordinary video for scalable injury-risk assessment. He also studies human-robot collaboration, designing and evaluating collaborative robots and shared-control schemes that allocate tasks to reduce ergonomic burden while preserving skilled manual performance. His application domains span manufacturing, hand-tool design, surgical and clinical motion analysis, and driver behavior, and his methods combine laboratory biomechanics, field exposure assessment, and quantitative modeling.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Robert G. Radwin, University of Wisconsin-Madison (Industrial and Systems Engineering / Biomedical Engineering; Occupational Ergonomics and Biomechanics Laboratory), AAAS Fellow 2025. Confirmed via Google Scholar profile (user=kk2kJSsAAAAJ; ~7,034 total citations, h-index 47), UW-Madison faculty/experts pages, ResearchGate, and consistency with the pre-verified Human Factors hf_papers (recurring co-authors Yu Hen Hu, Bilge Mutlu, Anna Konstant, Yin Li; sanding / HRC / computer-vision lifting themes). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref with matching titles, venues, and Radwin in the author list: recent_1 (10.1177/00187208261460709), recent_2 (10.1007/s00138-025-01758-w, Machine Vision and Applications, authors Zhu/Mu/Radwin/Li), recent_3 (10.1177/00187208241272066), seminal_1 (10.1177/001872088602800308, Human Factors 1986), seminal_2 (10.1109/TASE.2018.2789820, IEEE TASE 2018). Citation counts from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 ~341; seminal_2 ~236). A 2025 Ergonomics paper 'Automatic estimation of Hand Activity Level from upper-limb trajectories' appears on his Scholar profile but no DOI could be verified, so it was excluded in favor of the Crossref-verified Machine Vision and Applications 2025 paper. No unverifiable citations are included in the output.
